description: The shortfin mako is the world's fastest shark, capable of sustained speeds and spectacular leaps out of the water when hooked or pursuing prey. Found in open ocean worldwide in tropical and temperate seas, it is a highly migratory apex predator. Despite its speed and power, it has suffered steep population declines from bycatch and targeted fishing for its meat and fins.
funFact: The shortfin mako can reach burst speeds of up to 45 mph (72 km/h) and has been recorded leaping over 6 m (20 ft) into the air — making it not only the fastest shark but one of the most acrobatic fish in the ocean.
habitat: Open ocean in tropical and temperate seas worldwide, from the surface to depths of at least 500 m. A highly pelagic species that rarely enters inshore waters; prefers surface temperatures of 17–22°C but can range into cooler regions.
diet: An aggressive apex predator feeding primarily on fast-moving bony fish — especially bluefish, swordfish, and tuna — as well as squid and other sharks. Its speed and needle-like teeth are specifically adapted for seizing slippery, high-velocity prey.
size: Typically 1.8–3.2 m (6–10.5 ft); max confirmed ~4.45 m (14.6 ft). Females grow significantly larger than males.

vitalSigns.label: Diet
vitalSigns.value: Fast-moving bony fish (bluefish, swordfish, tuna), squid, and other sharks

vitalSigns.label: Population Growth Rate
vitalSigns.value: Very low — females don't mature until ~18–21 years of age

vitalSigns.label: Key Adaptations
vitalSigns.value: Regional endothermy (warm-bloodedness); crescent-shaped lunate tail for efficient high-speed propulsion; counter-current heat exchange in muscles

habitatStats.label: Global Range
habitatStats.value: Open ocean worldwide in tropical and temperate seas, typically between 50°N and 50°S

habitatStats.label: Depth Range
habitatStats.value: Surface to ~500 m (1,640 ft); performs vertical migrations into deeper, cooler water

threats.name: Bycatch
threats.description: The leading cause of mortality. Makos are among the most commonly caught sharks in Atlantic and Pacific pelagic longline fisheries targeting tuna and swordfish. Vast quantities have been removed from ocean basins since the 1950s.

threats.name: Targeted Fishing
threats.description: Actively targeted for its meat (sometimes sold as 'swordfish' or 'flake' in markets) and fins. ICCAT imposed a no-retention measure for North Atlantic mako in 2019, but compliance and enforcement remain inconsistent.

threats.name: Extremely Late Female Maturity
threats.description: Female shortfin makos don't reproduce until around 18–21 years old — one of the latest maturation ages of any shark species. This makes recovery from overfishing extraordinarily slow.

threats.name: Climate Change
threats.description: Shifts in prey distributions and increasing sea surface temperatures alter migration patterns and compress the thermal range available to a species adapted to specific temperature bands.

actionItems.title: Support North Atlantic No-Retention Rules
actionItems.description: Advocate for full compliance with ICCAT's mako no-retention measures in the North Atlantic, and for similar protections to be extended to all ocean basins.

actionItems.title: Choose Sustainable Seafood
actionItems.description: Avoid purchasing fish labeled generically as 'shark steak' or 'flake'. Use the Seafood Watch guide to identify sustainably caught alternatives to shark meat.

actionItems.title: Support Mako Research and Tagging
actionItems.description: Donate to organizations funding longline observer programs and satellite tagging research that tracks mako migrations and measures fishing mortality rates.

actionItems.title: Advocate for CITES Appendix II Enforcement
actionItems.description: Shortfin makos are listed on CITES Appendix II. Support stronger trade documentation requirements and enforcement to stop unreported landings and illegal fin trading.

healthMetrics.label: Prey Availability
healthMetrics.value: Declining — swordfish and tuna heavily fished

healthMetrics.label: Fishing Pressure
healthMetrics.value: Critical — top bycatch species in pelagic longlines

healthMetrics.label: Protection Coverage
healthMetrics.value: Partial — CITES II; N. Atlantic no-retention (ICCAT 2019)

populationTrendMeta.note: Derived from ICCAT's 2019 North Atlantic stock assessment, extrapolated globally. Historical values are modeled from fishing mortality rates; all figures carry high uncertainty.

preyDeclineRegions.name: N. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Bluefish, swordfish, and bluefin tuna heavily overfished

preyDeclineRegions.name: Mediterranean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Bluefin near collapse; swordfish severely depleted

preyDeclineRegions.name: N. Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Albacore and bigeye tuna under intense longline pressure

preyDeclineRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
preyDeclineRegions.note: Pelagic fish stocks declining across the Indian and W. Pacific

preyDeclineRegions.name: S. Atlantic
preyDeclineRegions.note: Swordfish and tuna under moderate and growing pressure

preyDeclineRegions.name: Southern Ocean
preyDeclineRegions.note: Prey relatively abundant; makos at range fringe

fishingPressureRegions.name: N. Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: ICCAT no-retention (2019); decades of prior overfishing

fishingPressureRegions.name: Mediterranean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Dense longlines; illegal landings widespread; no enforcement

fishingPressureRegions.name: Indo-Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: Heavy longline effort; no basin-wide mako protections

fishingPressureRegions.name: N. Pacific
fishingPressureRegions.note: California Current and tropical longlines; common bycatch

fishingPressureRegions.name: S. Atlantic
fishingPressureRegions.note: Expanding distant-water fleets; no S. Atlantic protections

fishingPressureRegions.name: Southern Ocean
fishingPressureRegions.note: Some bycatch in toothfish longlines; reduced pressure
